segments.title: Iron Duke as Jellicoe's flagship
segments.summary: The Iron Duke class combined 13.5-inch guns, a 12-inch belt, and 21-knot performance into the balanced flagship Admiral Jellicoe chose to retain.
segments.body: Clarke presents Iron Duke as the mature end of Britain's 13.5-inch-gun battleship development. Its gun battery, protection, range, and 21.4-knot speed made it a capable command ship rather than simply an older design waiting to be replaced. Jellicoe's decision to remain in Iron Duke illustrates how a well-balanced ship could remain useful beside newer classes.

evidence.note: The video gives Iron Duke's guns, boilers, speed, belt, range, and Jellicoe flagship discussion.

segments.title: Nevada and the American standard battleship
segments.summary: Nevada's 14-inch battery and triple-turret experiment marked the arrival of the American standard battleship, while its machinery comparison with Oklahoma exposed propulsion trade-offs.
segments.body: Clarke contrasts Nevada's ten 14-inch guns with the Iron Duke's smaller-calibre battery, showing why the American design drew attention despite having a similar gun count. He then compares Nevada's turbine machinery with Oklahoma's triple-expansion engines, treating speed and endurance claims as practical engineering questions rather than paper figures alone. The thick armour belt reinforces his view that the class was among the stronger battleship designs of its moment.

evidence.note: The video discusses Nevada's 14-inch guns, triple turret, Nevada and Oklahoma machinery, range, and armour belt.

segments.title: Derfflinger's uncertain return on investment
segments.summary: The Derfflinger class finally brought 12-inch guns to German battlecruisers, but Clarke questions whether its armament and fittings justified the cost against stronger foreign designs.
segments.body: Clarke notes the welcome move from 11-inch to eight 12-inch guns and the improved firing arcs, then asks what genuine capability the class added. Changes to secondary armament and torpedo tubes sat beside an armour scheme much like earlier German battlecruisers. His comparison turns the design into a question of strategic return: expensive capital ships needed to deliver more than incremental specification changes.

evidence.note: The video evaluates Derfflinger's 12-inch armament, firing arrangement, secondary battery, torpedo tubes, armour, cost, and capability.

segments.title: Borodino's fast battleship role
segments.summary: Russia's Borodino class paired twelve 14-inch guns with fast-battleship speed for the demanding routes from the Black and Baltic seas.
segments.body: Clarke places Borodino on the fast-battleship spectrum and connects that choice to Russia's need to fight through constrained exits before reaching wider waters. Four triple turrets gave it twelve 14-inch guns, a broadside he contrasts sharply with Derfflinger's eight 12-inch weapons. The comparison shows how strategic geography and a navy's preferred fighting role could produce a very different capital-ship answer.

evidence.note: The video links Borodino to Russian operating geography and discusses its triple turrets, twelve 14-inch guns, speed, armour, and comparison with Derfflinger.

segments.title: Queen Elizabeth raises the calibre stakes
segments.summary: Queen Elizabeth's eight 15-inch guns and 24-knot speed show Britain answering a wider 14-inch-gun race, not merely matching Germany.
segments.body: Clarke interprets Britain's move from 13.5-inch to 15-inch guns as a response to the wider field of navies adopting 14-inch weapons. The class coupled eight 15-inch guns with 75,000 shaft horsepower, a 13-inch belt, and a 24-knot top speed. That combination narrowed the comfortable speed margin German battlecruisers expected to enjoy while preserving battleship protection.

evidence.note: The video explains the 15-inch-gun step, 24 boilers, power, belt, speed, and the effect on battlecruiser speed advantage.

segments.title: Andrea Doria and a navy built for its waters
segments.summary: Italy's Andrea Doria class kept 12-inch guns and 21-knot speed because its design matched Mediterranean priorities rather than a direct North Sea contest.
segments.body: Clarke treats the Andrea Doria class as a coherent response to the opponents and waters Italy expected to face. Its 12-inch guns, 21-knot speed, and protection were adequate to that task even as other navies pursued larger-calibre weapons. He contrasts this clear fit between mission and design with his uncertainty about what German capital ships were meant to accomplish.

evidence.note: The video describes Andrea Doria's armament, speed, protection, intended fighting areas, and contrast with German design purpose.

segments.title: The 1912 balance of dreadnought fleets
segments.summary: A 1912 fleet count shows the Royal Navy pulling ahead while the United States, France, Italy, Japan, and several smaller powers continued to add dreadnoughts.
segments.body: Clarke uses ships in service and under construction to show that Britain retained a substantial and widening lead over Germany. The tally also places French, Italian, Austro-Hungarian, Brazilian, Japanese, American, and other programmes in the same picture. Counting hulls is therefore a separate strategic problem from comparing the newest gun or armour specification.

evidence.note: The video surveys dreadnought and battlecruiser numbers in service and construction across the major naval powers.

segments.title: A two-level global naval race
segments.summary: The 1912 naval race combined Germany's challenge in hull numbers with an American technological challenge that pushed Britain toward larger guns.
segments.body: Clarke rejects a simple Anglo-German account of the dreadnought race. Germany's fleet-building programme threatened Britain's numerical position, while American 14-inch battleships made a purely German comparison inadequate for British designers. Britain therefore had to preserve both a numerical margin and a technological one, which helps explain why Queen Elizabeth answered more than one rival.

evidence.note: The video distinguishes the hull-number competition from technological competition and links American 14-inch guns to Britain's 15-inch response.
